architectureFigure.caption: The final few-shot prompt improved upon previous steps. Changes with regard to the zero-shot prompt are marked in red: severity scale; a detailed explanation of error categories; an example of Fluency; and an example and value instruction of Omission (since the 'marked text' should come from the source sentence). Here for figure readability, we removed some quotation marks in the pseudo-JSON strings.

authors: Sidi Wang, Sophie Arnoult, Amir Kamran

breakthroughAssessment.justification: This paper presents a solid methodological contribution in prompt engineering for generating synthetic MTQE annotations. The PPbMQM approach, with its simplified MQM scheme and severity scaling, effectively leverages LLMs to produce data competitive with human annotations for training downstream QE models. This addresses a practical challenge (cost of LLM inference and human annotation) and provides a systematic way to create structured training data. While it doesn't introduce a fundamentally new model architecture or theory, its detailed empirical validation and focus on practical application in a complex annotation task make it a strong and useful contribution to the field. It improves upon prior LLM-based annotation efforts by providing a more robust and controllable method, especially for lower-quality segments.

coreProblem.concreteExample: Instead of directly applying an expensive LLM to perform Machine Translation Quality Estimation (MTQE), the problem is how to efficiently use LLMs to generate high-quality, structured MQM-style annotations. This synthetic data can then be used to train a cheaper, faster QE model like COMET, especially when human annotations are costly, time-consuming, or suffer from low inter-annotator agreement.
coreProblem.statement: While Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate strong performance in Machine Translation Quality Estimation (MTQE), their high inference costs hinder direct application. The existing Multidimensional Quality Metric (MQM) annotations are complex to operationalize, making it challenging to leverage LLMs effectively for generating high-quality training data for learned QE metrics like COMET.
whyItMatters: Reduces the high inference costs of directly applying LLMs for MTQE.
Provides interpretable error annotations (type, severity, span) which are crucial for post-editing and system improvement.
Enables training of competitive learned QE metrics (e.g., COMET) on synthetic, structured data, democratizing access to high-quality QE models.
Addresses the challenges of low inter-annotator agreement in human MQM annotations for fine-grained linguistic tasks.

evaluationHighlights: GPT-4o, when prompted with PPbMQM, produced annotations that significantly improved span, severity, and error-type F1 metrics compared to its zero-shot performance.
COMET models trained on PPbMQM annotations achieved higher Pearson correlation with human annotations than baseline models, especially in lower-quality segments (<0.8 quality score).
The synthetic training data generated by PPbMQM enabled COMET models to perform on par with models trained on costly human annotations for Chinese-English and English-German QE tasks.

evaluationSetup.setting: Segment-level Machine Translation Quality Estimation for Chinese-English (zh-en) and English-German (en-de) language pairs, involving LLM knowledge testing, prompt development, and training COMET QE models.

keyResultGroups.title: LLM Knowledge of QE and MQM

mainTakeaways: LLMs (especially GPT-4o and LLaMA 3) demonstrate strong knowledge of QE and MQM annotation schemes, outperforming simpler models like GPT-3.5.

The proposed few-shot PPbMQM prompting approach, which incorporates detailed error explanations and a severity scaling method, improves span metrics (F1 for span, severity, type) for LLM-generated annotations compared to zero-shot prompting.

COMET models trained on PPbMQM-generated annotations achieve competitive performance compared to models trained on human annotations, particularly showing stronger correlation in lower-quality segments for both Chinese-English and English-German.

LLMs tend to be stricter annotators than humans, overpredicting errors, but this behavior can be effectively mitigated by implementing a severity scaling method and selectively discarding low-severity annotations.

institutions: Maastricht university, Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam, Taus

explanation: Introduces a few-shot Prompt-Pattern-based-MQM (PPbMQM) prompt specifically designed to guide LLMs in generating annotations restricted to top-level MQM categories (Accuracy, Fluency, Terminology, Style, Locale Convention) and an added "Omission" category.

Implements a novel severity scaling method where LLMs assign a score from 1-5, which is then mapped to Major/Minor errors, rather than directly predicting Major/Minor. This helps to better control LLMs' tendency to overpredict errors and improves correlation with human judgments.

Demonstrates the practical utility of LLM-generated annotations as synthetic training data for fine-tuning learned QE metrics (like COMET), showing competitive performance with models trained on human annotations, particularly for lower-quality segments where LLM annotations sometimes yield better results.
keyNovelty.main: A systematic, multi-step prompt engineering approach (PPbMQM) to guide Large Language Models in generating simplified MQM-style annotations for Machine Translation Quality Estimation, incorporating persona, reflection, and output automater patterns, alongside a novel severity scaling method.

keyTerms.definition: The task of automatically assessing the quality of a machine translation without human intervention or reference translations.
A hierarchical scheme for annotating machine translation errors, classifying them by type, severity, and span.
A neural metric for evaluating machine translation quality, often used for quality estimation when trained on human judgment data.
The process of designing and refining input prompts for large language models to elicit desired outputs or behaviors.

prerequisites: Basic understanding of Machine Translation (MT) concepts.
Familiarity with Quality Estimation (QE) principles and existing metrics.
Knowledge of Large Language Models (LLMs) and their capabilities.
Understanding of fundamental Natural Language Processing (NLP) evaluation metrics (e.g., F1-score, Pearson correlation, Spearman correlation, Kendall Tau).

comparisonToPriorWork: Unlike Fernandes et al. (2023) who prompted PaLM models to output full Multidimensional Quality Metric (MQM) annotations, this work simplifies the MQM scheme to top-level categories and adds an "Omission" subcategory to simplify the task for LLMs.

This method introduces a severity scaling approach (LLMs predict 1-5 score, then mapped to Major/Minor) to address LLMs' tendency to overpredict errors and improve correlation with human judgments, contrasting with prior work that might discard error types or rely solely on severity.

The paper focuses on providing a systematic and comprehensive prompt engineering approach for training downstream QE models, rather than directly aiming to outperform existing direct LLM prompting methods for QE (e.g., GEMBA by Kocmi and Federmann, 2023b).
limitations: Potential data leakage: The test and development datasets used for evaluation might have been included in the training data of the proprietary LLMs, which could inflate performance metrics.

Limited domain coverage: The training data for downstream QE models only covered two language pairs (Chinese-English and English-German) and specific domains (news, e-commerce, conversation, social media), limiting generalizability to other domains or language pairs.

Single initialization for COMET training: COMET models were trained with only one initialization, which might not be sufficient for robust statistical testing, and future work should test with multiple initializations to strengthen experimental evidence.

modeling.label: MQM Severity Mapping
modeling.value: LLMs are prompted to assign a severity score on a scale of 1-5 (least severe to most severe). Scores 4-5 are mapped to "Major" errors, and scores 1-3 are mapped to "Minor" errors. For the final QE model training, annotations with severity scores 1-2 were discarded, and severity-3 scores were mapped to the "Minor" category.

modeling.label: Quality Score Calculation
modeling.value: Segment-level quality scores are derived from MQM annotations by weighing major errors by 5 and minor errors by 1, following prior work (Freitag et al., 2021a).

modeling.label: Evaluation Metrics
modeling.value: Span, Severity, and Error-type F1 metrics are used to evaluate the agreement between LLM-generated and human annotations at the span level. Pearson \rho, Spearman r, and Kendall \tau correlations are used to assess segment-level quality score agreement with human judgments.

pipelineFlow: LLM Knowledge Testing: Initially, four LLMs (GPT-3.5, GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4o, LLaMA 3) are tested on their knowledge of Quality Estimation (QE) and Multidimensional Quality Metric (MQM) by asking related questions.

Zero-shot Prompting Development: An initial zero-shot prompt is designed to elicit MQM-style annotations. Issues like inconsistent error span indexing are identified and resolved (e.g., by using NLTK tokenizer).

Few-shot Prompting Development: The prompt is further refined by adding detailed explanations for each error type, an example for the 'Omission' error category, and a comma splice 'Fluency' error example. A severity scoring method (1-5 scale mapped to Major/Minor) is introduced.

Annotation Generation: The developed few-shot PPbMQM prompt is applied using GPT-4o to generate a large dataset of MQM-style annotations for Chinese-English (20703 segments) and English-German (10121 segments).

Downstream QE Model Training: Reference-free QE models (COMET) are trained using the LLM-generated annotations. Their performance is then evaluated and compared against models trained on human MQM annotations on randomly selected test segments.

problemDefinition.label: Machine Translation Quality Estimation (MTQE)
problemDefinition.value: The task of automatically assessing the quality of a machine translation, often involving the identification of error spans, their types, and severity, without relying on human judgments or reference translations during inference.

problemDefinition.label: MQM Annotation Scheme
problemDefinition.value: A refined hierarchical error typology for classifying machine translation errors. This paper uses a simplified version, restricting categories to Accuracy, Fluency, Terminology, Style, Locale Convention, and an added 'Omission' subcategory, with two severity levels (Major, Minor).

technicalDetails.reproducibility: The authors state that the GitHub repository for COMET (https://github.com/Unbabel/COMET) was used for generating training data. The specific prompts used for the LLMs are detailed in the appendix. Proprietary LLMs (OpenAI and Replicate APIs) were used, for which the source of training data is unknown. Stability analyses of GPT-4o with different prompt variants and system fingerprints are provided in the appendix, showing stable behavior.

systemModules.details: Utilizes LLMs such as GPT-3.5, GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4o, and LLaMA 3 70B for initial experiments and prompt refinement, with GPT-4o selected as the primary annotator for large-scale annotation generation.
systemModules.name: Large Language Models (LLMs)
systemModules.role: Core annotator for generating MQM-style error annotations from source and target sentences.

systemModules.details: A comprehensive set of instructions, examples, and constraints for LLMs. It incorporates persona, reflection, and output automater patterns. It guides LLMs to identify translation errors, assign specific top-level MQM error types (plus Omission), and provide severity scores (1-5), which are then mapped to Major/Minor categories.
systemModules.name: PPbMQM Prompt
systemModules.role: Guides the LLM's annotation process to generate structured and simplified MQM annotations that align with human judgment principles.

systemModules.details: Integrated into the prompt instructions to ensure consistent identification and indexing of error spans within the target sentence, resolving issues encountered in initial zero-shot prompting.
systemModules.name: NLTK Tokenizer (Instruction)
systemModules.role: Ensures consistent error span indexing for structured output generation by the LLM.

systemModules.details: A reference-free Machine Translation Quality Estimation model, which is fine-tuned using the large-scale synthetic MQM annotations generated by the LLMs. This model then performs QE without further LLM inference.
systemModules.name: COMET-QE Model
systemModules.role: Downstream quality estimation model trained on LLM-generated synthetic data to provide efficient and competitive QE.

thesis: This paper proposes a systematic prompt engineering approach, called PPbMQM, for Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate simplified MQM-style annotations for Machine Translation Quality Estimation. These LLM-generated annotations correlate well with human judgments and can effectively train COMET models to achieve competitive segment-level QE performance for Chinese-English and English-German language pairs.
title: Large Language Models as Annotators for Machine Translation Quality Estimation
